section.title: The Fall
section.sectionRationale: This is the hinge of the toledot of the heavens and the earth, and of the whole Bible. The harmony of chapter two ruptures: a crafty serpent, a broken command, judgment, and, in the same breath, the first promise of a rescuer.
section.thesis: Distrust of God's word fractures the good world; yet in the wreckage God seeks the guilty, promises a seed who will crush the serpent, and clothes their shame.
section.frameNote: Five scenes: the deception; God seeking the hiding pair; the serpent's fate and the first promise; the judgments; and grace in the exile.

media.alt: The tree, its fruit, and the serpent
media.caption: The tempter twists God's word beside the forbidden tree.

verses.text: Now the {{serpent|serpent}} was more crafty than any beast of the field that the LORD God had made. And he said to the woman, “Did God really say, ‘You must not eat from any tree in the garden?’”
The woman answered the serpent, “We may eat the fruit of the trees of the garden,
but about the fruit of the tree in the middle of the garden, God has said, ‘You must not eat of it or touch it, or you will die.’”
“You will not surely die,” the serpent told the woman.
“For God knows that in the day you eat of it, your eyes will be opened and you will be like God, knowing good and evil.”

When the woman saw that the tree was good for food and pleasing to the eyes, and that it was desirable for obtaining wisdom, she took the fruit and ate it. She also gave some to her husband who was with her, and he ate it.
And the eyes of both of them were opened, and they knew that they were naked; so they sewed together fig leaves and made coverings for themselves.
Then the man and his wife heard the voice of the LORD God walking in the garden in the breeze of the day, and they hid themselves from the presence of the LORD God among the trees of the garden.
But the LORD God called out to the man, “Where are you?”
“I heard Your voice in the garden,” he replied, “and I was afraid because I was naked; so I hid myself.”
“Who told you that you were naked?” asked the LORD God. “Have you eaten from the tree of which I commanded you not to eat?”
And the man answered, “The woman whom You gave me, she gave me fruit from the tree, and I ate it.”
Then the LORD God said to the woman, “What is this you have done?” “The serpent deceived me,” she replied, “and I ate.”

units.label: The serpent's fate and the first promise

verses.text: So the LORD God said to the serpent: “Because you have done this, cursed are you above all livestock and every beast of the field! On your belly will you go, and dust you will eat, all the days of your life.
And I will put {{enmity|enmity}} between you and the woman, and between your {{seed|seed}} and her seed. He will crush your head, and you will strike his heel.”
To the woman He said: “I will sharply increase your pain in childbirth; in pain you will bring forth children. Your desire will be for your husband, and he will rule over you.”

And to Adam He said: “Because you have listened to the voice of your wife and have eaten from the tree of which I commanded you not to eat, cursed is the ground because of you; through toil you will eat of it all the days of your life.
Both thorns and thistles it will yield for you, and you will eat the plants of the field.
By the sweat of your brow you will eat your bread, until you return to the ground, because out of it were you taken. For dust you are, and to dust you shall return.”

media.alt: A flaming sword guarding the way to the tree of life
media.caption: Clothed by God, yet barred from the tree of life by a flaming sword.

verses.text: And Adam named his wife {{eve|Eve}}, because she would be the mother of all the living.
And the LORD God made garments of skin for Adam and his wife, and He clothed them.
Then the LORD God said, “Behold, the man has become like one of Us, knowing good and evil. And now, lest he reach out his hand and take also from the tree of life, and eat, and live forever...”
Therefore the LORD God banished him from the Garden of Eden to work the ground from which he had been taken.
So He drove out the man and stationed cherubim on the east side of the Garden of Eden, along with a whirling sword of flame to guard the way to the tree of life.

serpent.gloss: A serpent or snake. Here it is the crafty tempter; later Scripture identifies the ancient serpent as the devil and Satan, Revelation 12:9.

enmity.gloss: Deep hostility, open war. God Himself sets enmity between the serpent and the woman's line; the rescue of the world begins as a declaration of war on evil.

seed.gloss: Offspring or seed. The word can mean many descendants or narrow to one; across Scripture 'her seed' focuses to the one Christ who crushes the serpent, Galatians 3:16, Romans 16:20.

eve.gloss: The name Eve sounds like the Hebrew for living. In the very hour death enters, the man names his wife the mother of all the living, a small act of hope in God's promise.

basic.context: This is the hinge of the whole Bible. The good world God made is broken here. A crafty serpent tempts the woman, the man joins her, and they disobey the one command. So much of the pain in the world flows from this moment. But even here God makes a promise to put it right.
basic.hermeneutics: The chapter really happened, and it explains the world we live in: why there is shame, blame, pain, and death. People debate how much is pictured in symbols, but the point stands, humanity turned from God and fell. Right in the middle, in verse 15, God promises a rescuer.
basic.originalLanguages: The tempter is the nachash, the serpent, later shown to be the devil. God sets enmity, war, between the serpent and the woman's seed, her offspring. That word seed narrows across the Bible to one person, Jesus. And the man names his wife Eve, which sounds like living, a seed of hope.
basic.typology: The garments of skin God makes point forward to sacrifice: something dies so the guilty can be covered. The promised seed who crushes the serpent points to Christ. The flaming sword that guards Eden is answered when Jesus opens the way back to the tree of life.
basic.christ: Verse 15 is the first announcement of the gospel: a descendant of the woman will crush the serpent's head, though He is struck in the doing of it. That is the cross and the resurrection. Paul says God will soon crush Satan under our feet, and Revelation shows the serpent finally defeated.
basic.god: God is holy and just: sin has real consequences. But He is also gracious: He seeks the hiding pair, He clothes them, and He promises a Saviour before He sends them out. Even His judgment is laced with mercy.
basic.crossReferences: Romans 5 says sin and death entered through Adam. Revelation 12 names the serpent as the devil. Romans 16 echoes the crushing of Satan. Revelation 22 reopens the tree of life.
basic.oneStory: This is the fall, the fracture the rest of the Bible works to heal. From the wreckage God speaks the first promise of a rescuer, and the whole story from here runs toward the seed of the woman, Jesus, who undoes what happened in the garden.

context.literary: The turning point of the toledot of the heavens and the earth, and of the Bible. It moves from the harmony of chapter two to rupture, told in tight dialogue and then a series of judgments in poetic form.
context.historical: For Israel, freshly given God's law, this explains why the good creation is now full of thorns, pain, and death, and why they, like Adam, are so prone to disobey.
context.canonical: The fountainhead of sin and death, the problem that creation, covenant, Christ, and new creation all address. Genesis 3:15 is often called the protoevangelium, the first gospel.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To reveal how sin entered the good world through distrust of God's word, to show the just consequences, and to plant the first promise of redemption.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The narrative describes the fall and its judgments; it is not prescribing them. The abiding truths are the reality of sin, the justice of God, and the promise of a rescuer.

debates.question: Who or what is the serpent?

views.label: A literal serpent
views.case: The text presents a real creature, the craftiest beast of the field.

views.label: Satan through the serpent
views.case: Later Scripture, Revelation 12:9, names the ancient serpent as the devil, so most read Satan speaking through it.

debates.bearing: Either way the tempter is real and the fall is real; the New Testament reads the devil behind the serpent.

study.originalLanguages: Distrust begins with a twist of God's word, 'Did God really say'. The serpent, nachash, flatly denies the warning. God answers with enmity and a promised seed who will crush the serpent's head, and the man names his wife Eve, living, holding on to that promise even as death enters.

typology.fulfillment: An animal dies so the guilty are covered, a first shadow of the atonement fulfilled at the cross.

typology.type: The seed of the woman, Genesis 3:15
typology.fulfillment: Christ, born of a woman, who crushes the serpent through His death and resurrection.

typology.type: The flaming sword guarding the tree of life
typology.fulfillment: The way back to life, reopened in Christ and consummated in Revelation 22.

study.christ: Genesis 3:15, the protoevangelium, promises a descendant of the woman who will crush the serpent's head while being struck Himself. The New Testament sees this fulfilled in Jesus: born of a woman, Galatians 4:4; appearing to destroy the works of the devil, 1 John 3:8; and crushing Satan under His people's feet, Romans 16:20. The garments of skin foreshadow the covering God provides in Christ.
study.god: God is just: the warned death comes, and the ground is cursed. Yet He is gracious throughout: He comes seeking, 'Where are you'; He speaks the first promise of salvation; He clothes their shame; and even the exile from the tree of life is a mercy, so they do not live forever in a fallen state.

crossReferences.note: Sin and death entered through Adam; grace and life come through Christ, the last Adam.
Names the ancient serpent as the devil and Satan.
God will soon crush Satan under your feet, echoing Genesis 3:15.
The tree of life, barred here, is restored in the new creation.

study.oneStory: This is the fall: the good world fractures through distrust of God. But in the same breath God speaks the first promise of a rescuer, the seed of the woman, and clothes the guilty. Every page after this runs toward that seed, Jesus, who crushes the serpent and reopens the way to the tree of life.

sermon.title: Paradise Lost (Genesis 3:8 to 24)
sermon.why: Keller works through the fall to answer what went wrong with the human race and how God begins to put it right, drawing out the grace and the first promise of the gospel in this chapter.

quiz.q: How did the serpent first lead the woman into sin?

options.text: By casting doubt on God's word and God's goodness, 'Did God really say'.
options.note: Temptation begins by twisting and doubting what God has said.

options.text: By offering fruit that looked good to eat.
options.note: The fruit's appeal is real, but the root was doubting God's word.

options.text: By forcing her to eat against her will.
options.note: She was deceived and chose; she was not forced.

options.text: By proving that God had lied.
options.note: God had not lied; the serpent lied, and the warned death came.

quiz.q: What is the first promise of the gospel in this chapter?

options.text: Genesis 3:15, a seed of the woman will crush the serpent's head.
options.note: This is the protoevangelium, the first announcement of Christ's victory.

options.text: That God would clothe them with garments of skin.
options.note: A true grace and a shadow of atonement, but the explicit promise is verse 15.

options.text: That childbirth would still continue.
options.note: A mercy within judgment, but not the gospel promise.

options.text: That there is no real hope after the fall.
options.note: The chapter is full of hope; God promises a rescuer.

quiz.q: How does God show grace even in judgment?

options.text: He seeks them, promises a Saviour, and clothes their shame before sending them out.
options.note: Mercy runs through the whole scene.

options.text: He lets them live rather than killing them at once.
options.note: True, but it understates the depth of grace, the promise and the covering.

options.text: He removes all the consequences of their sin.
options.note: He does not remove the consequences; sin is judged, yet mercy is shown.

options.text: He shows no grace at all, only anger.
options.note: The chapter is threaded with grace: seeking, promise, and covering.

quiz.q: Who is the seed of the woman, ultimately?

options.text: Jesus Christ, who crushes the serpent through His death and resurrection.
options.note: The New Testament identifies the seed as Christ, Galatians 3 and Romans 16:20.

options.text: The line of faithful people descended from Eve.
options.note: The seed runs through a line, but it narrows to one, Christ.

options.text: Any person who resists evil.
options.note: This misses that the promise centres on one appointed rescuer.

options.text: No one; it is only a symbol.
options.note: Scripture treats it as a real promise fulfilled in Jesus.

quiz.q: What went wrong in the fall, at its heart?

options.text: Humanity distrusted God and grasped to be like God apart from Him.
options.note: The root of sin is distrust and self-rule against God.

options.text: They broke a rule about a tree.
options.note: True on the surface, but the deeper issue was distrust of God Himself.

options.text: They simply made an honest mistake.
options.note: It was disobedience and distrust, not a mere slip.

options.text: God set them up to fail.
options.note: God provided abundantly and warned truthfully; the choice was theirs.

questions: Temptation still begins with 'Did God really say'. Where are you being tempted to doubt God's word or His goodness right now?
Adam blamed Eve, and Eve blamed the serpent. Where are you hiding, or shifting blame, instead of coming honestly to God?
God came seeking with 'Where are you'. How does it change things to know that God pursues sinners rather than waiting for them to clean themselves up first?
God covered their shame with garments He provided. What are you using to cover your own shame, and what would it mean to receive God's covering in Christ instead?
The crushing of the serpent is fulfilled in Jesus. How does knowing that evil is already defeated help you face temptation and suffering this week?

translationNotes.note: The same Hebrew root can be rendered crush, bruise, or strike, and it appears for both the head and the heel. The wordplay holds together decisive victory over the serpent and real cost to the rescuer.
Your desire will be for your husband can also be rendered you will desire to control your husband; either way it describes the conflict that now marks the relationship.
Eve sounds like the Hebrew for living or life; naming her so is an act of hope spoken right after the sentence of death.
